instruction: Implement a lock-free queue in C++ using atomic operations. Explain the ABA problem and how to prevent it. Provide both a single-producer/single-consumer and a multi-producer/multi-consumer version.
output:
<think>
Lock-free data structures avoid mutexes, using atomic operations (CAS - Compare-And-Swap) for thread safety. They guarantee system-wide progress: at least one thread makes progress even if others are suspended.

The SPSC (single-producer, single-consumer) queue is the simplest lock-free structure. It uses a ring buffer with separate head (consumer) and tail (producer) indices. Since only one thread writes each index, we only need atomic loads/stores with appropriate memory ordering — no CAS needed.

The MPMC (multi-producer, multi-consumer) queue is much harder. Multiple producers compete to write to the tail, and multiple consumers compete to read from the head. This requires CAS operations.

The ABA problem: thread 1 reads value A from an atomic, gets preempted. Thread 2 changes A to B then back to A. Thread 1 resumes, CAS sees A (unchanged), succeeds — but the data structure may have changed underneath. Solution: use a generation counter (packed with the pointer) that increments on every modification, so even if the value returns to A, the counter won't match.

For the MPMC queue, I'll use the Dmitry Vyukov approach with per-slot sequence numbers. Each slot has an atomic sequence counter. Producers and consumers check and advance their slot's sequence, providing lock-free progress.

Memory ordering is critical: acquire/release semantics ensure visibility of writes across threads. seq_cst is safest but slowest; relaxed is fastest but requires careful reasoning.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <cassert>
#include <cstddef>
#include <iostream>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

// === SPSC Lock-Free Queue (Ring Buffer) ===

template <typename T, size_t Capacity>
class SPSCQueue {
    static_assert(Capacity > 0 && (Capacity & (Capacity - 1)) == 0,
                  "Capacity must be a power of 2");

    alignas(64) std::atomic<size_t> head_{0};  // consumer reads here
    alignas(64) std::atomic<size_t> tail_{0};  // producer writes here
    T buffer_[Capacity];

public:
    bool push(const T& value) {
        const size_t tail = tail_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        const size_t next = (tail + 1) & (Capacity - 1);

        if (next == head_.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
            return false;  // Full
        }

        buffer_[tail] = value;
        tail_.store(next, std::memory_order_release);
        return true;
    }

    std::optional<T> pop() {
        const size_t head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);

        if (head == tail_.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
            return std::nullopt;  // Empty
        }

        T value = buffer_[head];
        head_.store((head + 1) & (Capacity - 1), std::memory_order_release);
        return value;
    }
};


// === MPMC Lock-Free Queue (Vyukov's bounded queue) ===

template <typename T, size_t Capacity>
class MPMCQueue {
    static_assert(Capacity > 0 && (Capacity & (Capacity - 1)) == 0,
                  "Capacity must be a power of 2");

    struct Slot {
        std::atomic<size_t> sequence;
        T data;
    };

    alignas(64) Slot slots_[Capacity];
    alignas(64) std::atomic<size_t> head_{0};
    alignas(64) std::atomic<size_t> tail_{0};

public:
    MPMCQueue() {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < Capacity; ++i) {
            slots_[i].sequence.store(i, std::memory_order_relaxed);
        }
    }

    bool push(const T& value) {
        size_t tail = tail_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);

        for (;;) {
            Slot& slot = slots_[tail & (Capacity - 1)];
            size_t seq = slot.sequence.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
            intptr_t diff = static_cast<intptr_t>(seq) - static_cast<intptr_t>(tail);

            if (diff == 0) {
                // Slot is ready for writing
                if (tail_.compare_exchange_weak(tail, tail + 1,
                        std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                    slot.data = value;
                    slot.sequence.store(tail + 1, std::memory_order_release);
                    return true;
                }
            } else if (diff < 0) {
                return false;  // Queue is full
            } else {
                // Another producer claimed this slot, retry
                tail = tail_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
            }
        }
    }

    std::optional<T> pop() {
        size_t head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);

        for (;;) {
            Slot& slot = slots_[head & (Capacity - 1)];
            size_t seq = slot.sequence.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
            intptr_t diff = static_cast<intptr_t>(seq) -
                           static_cast<intptr_t>(head + 1);

            if (diff == 0) {
                // Slot has data ready
                if (head_.compare_exchange_weak(head, head + 1,
                        std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                    T value = slot.data;
                    slot.sequence.store(head + Capacity,
                                       std::memory_order_release);
                    return value;
                }
            } else if (diff < 0) {
                return std::nullopt;  // Queue is empty
            } else {
                head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
            }
        }
    }
};


int main() {
    // Test SPSC queue
    SPSCQueue<int, 1024> spsc;
    std::atomic<int> sum{0};

    std::thread producer([&]() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 100000; ++i) {
            while (!spsc.push(i)) {} // spin until space
        }
    });

    std::thread consumer([&]() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 100000; ++i) {
            while (true) {
                auto val = spsc.pop();
                if (val) { sum += *val; break; }
            }
        }
    });

    producer.join();
    consumer.join();
    assert(sum == (99999LL * 100000LL / 2));
    std::cout << "SPSC test PASS (sum=" << sum << ")" << std::endl;

    // Test MPMC queue
    MPMCQueue<int, 1024> mpmc;
    std::atomic<long long> mpmc_sum{0};
    constexpr int N = 50000;
    constexpr int NUM_PRODUCERS = 4;
    constexpr int NUM_CONSUMERS = 4;

    std::vector<std::thread> threads;

    for (int p = 0; p < NUM_PRODUCERS; ++p) {
        threads.emplace_back([&, p]() {
            for (int i = p * N; i < (p + 1) * N; ++i) {
                while (!mpmc.push(i)) {}
            }
        });
    }

    for (int c = 0; c < NUM_CONSUMERS; ++c) {
        threads.emplace_back([&]() {
            for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
                while (true) {
                    auto val = mpmc.pop();
                    if (val) { mpmc_sum += *val; break; }
                }
            }
        });
    }

    for (auto& t : threads) t.join();

    long long expected = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < NUM_PRODUCERS * N; ++i) expected += i;
    assert(mpmc_sum == expected);
    std::cout << "MPMC test PASS (sum=" << mpmc_sum << ")" << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement memory-mapped I/O in C++ for high-performance file processing. Show how to use mmap (or platform equivalent) safely, handle page faults, and compare performance with traditional read/write.
output:
<think>
Memory-mapped I/O (mmap) maps a file directly into the process's virtual address space, allowing file access through pointer dereference instead of read()/write() system calls. Benefits: zero-copy access, kernel handles paging, and the OS's page cache is shared.

Key considerations:
1. mmap returns a void* to the mapped region. Access triggers page faults that load data from disk transparently.
2. Must unmap with munmap when done. File descriptor can be closed after mapping.
3. For writing: use MAP_SHARED to write back to file, MAP_PRIVATE for copy-on-write.
4. Page alignment: offset must be page-aligned. Size can extend past file end (but accessing past end is UB).
5. Error handling: mmap can fail (MAP_FAILED), page faults on invalid regions cause SIGBUS.
6. Large files: mmap is excellent because only accessed pages are loaded into memory.

Performance comparison:
- Sequential read: mmap and read() are similar (both use page cache)
- Random access: mmap wins significantly (no system call overhead per access)
- Small files: read() can be faster (mmap has setup overhead)
- Very large files: mmap shines (only active pages in memory)

For Windows compatibility, I'd use CreateFileMapping/MapViewOfFile, but I'll focus on POSIX for the primary implementation with notes on Windows equivalents.

I'll implement a RAII wrapper for mmap that handles cleanup, a word counter using mmap (demonstrating the pointer-based access pattern), and a benchmark comparing mmap vs fread.
</think>

```cpp
#include <sys/mman.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <chrono>
#include <fstream>
#include <string>
#include <stdexcept>

// === RAII Memory-Mapped File ===

class MappedFile {
    void* data_ = nullptr;
    size_t size_ = 0;
    int fd_ = -1;

public:
    MappedFile(const std::string& path, bool writable = false) {
        int flags = writable ? O_RDWR : O_RDONLY;
        fd_ = open(path.c_str(), flags);
        if (fd_ < 0) {
            throw std::runtime_error("Failed to open: " + path);
        }

        struct stat st;
        if (fstat(fd_, &st) < 0) {
            close(fd_);
            throw std::runtime_error("Failed to stat: " + path);
        }
        size_ = static_cast<size_t>(st.st_size);

        if (size_ == 0) {
            close(fd_);
            return; // Empty file — valid but no mapping
        }

        int prot = PROT_READ | (writable ? PROT_WRITE : 0);
        int mflags = writable ? MAP_SHARED : MAP_PRIVATE;

        data_ = mmap(nullptr, size_, prot, mflags, fd_, 0);
        if (data_ == MAP_FAILED) {
            close(fd_);
            throw std::runtime_error("mmap failed for: " + path);
        }

        // Hint to kernel: sequential access pattern
        madvise(data_, size_, MADV_SEQUENTIAL);
    }

    ~MappedFile() {
        if (data_ && data_ != MAP_FAILED) {
            munmap(data_, size_);
        }
        if (fd_ >= 0) {
            close(fd_);
        }
    }

    // Non-copyable, movable
    MappedFile(const MappedFile&) = delete;
    MappedFile& operator=(const MappedFile&) = delete;
    MappedFile(MappedFile&& other) noexcept
        : data_(other.data_), size_(other.size_), fd_(other.fd_) {
        other.data_ = nullptr;
        other.size_ = 0;
        other.fd_ = -1;
    }

    const char* data() const { return static_cast<const char*>(data_); }
    char* mutable_data() { return static_cast<char*>(data_); }
    size_t size() const { return size_; }

    void sync() {
        if (data_ && size_ > 0) {
            msync(data_, size_, MS_SYNC);
        }
    }
};


// === Word counter using mmap (zero-copy) ===

size_t count_words_mmap(const std::string& path) {
    MappedFile file(path);
    if (file.size() == 0) return 0;

    const char* data = file.data();
    size_t len = file.size();
    size_t count = 0;
    bool in_word = false;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < len; ++i) {
        bool is_space = (data[i] == ' ' || data[i] == '\n' ||
                         data[i] == '\t' || data[i] == '\r');
        if (!is_space && !in_word) {
            ++count;
            in_word = true;
        } else if (is_space) {
            in_word = false;
        }
    }
    return count;
}


// === Word counter using fread (traditional I/O) ===

size_t count_words_fread(const std::string& path) {
    FILE* f = fopen(path.c_str(), "rb");
    if (!f) throw std::runtime_error("Failed to open: " + path);

    char buf[65536];
    size_t count = 0;
    bool in_word = false;

    while (size_t n = fread(buf, 1, sizeof(buf), f)) {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            bool is_space = (buf[i] == ' ' || buf[i] == '\n' ||
                             buf[i] == '\t' || buf[i] == '\r');
            if (!is_space && !in_word) {
                ++count;
                in_word = true;
            } else if (is_space) {
                in_word = false;
            }
        }
    }
    fclose(f);
    return count;
}


// === Benchmark ===

template <typename Func>
double benchmark(Func&& f, int iterations = 3) {
    auto best = std::chrono::duration<double>::max();
    for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        f();
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        auto dur = std::chrono::duration<double>(end - start);
        if (dur < best) best = dur;
    }
    return best.count();
}


int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    if (argc < 2) {
        // Create a test file
        std::ofstream out("/tmp/mmap_test.txt");
        for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; ++i) {
            out << "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog\n";
        }
        out.close();
        argv[1] = const_cast<char*>("/tmp/mmap_test.txt");
    }

    std::string path = argv[1];
    std::cout << "File: " << path << std::endl;

    size_t words_mmap = 0, words_fread = 0;

    double t_mmap = benchmark([&]() {
        words_mmap = count_words_mmap(path);
    });

    double t_fread = benchmark([&]() {
        words_fread = count_words_fread(path);
    });

    std::cout << "mmap:  " << words_mmap << " words in "
              << t_mmap * 1000 << " ms" << std::endl;
    std::cout << "fread: " << words_fread << " words in "
              << t_fread * 1000 << " ms" << std::endl;
    std::cout << "Speedup: " << t_fread / t_mmap << "x" << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a custom memory allocator in C++ that provides pool allocation for fixed-size objects. Include thread-safe allocation, memory alignment, and debug features like leak detection and double-free detection.
output:
<think>
Custom allocators are essential in performance-critical C++ code (game engines, embedded systems, HFT) where standard malloc/new has unacceptable overhead or fragmentation.

A pool allocator pre-allocates a large block and carves it into fixed-size chunks. Allocation is O(1) — just pop from a free list. Deallocation is O(1) — push back to the free list. Zero fragmentation since all allocations are the same size.

Design:
1. Pre-allocate a large block divided into fixed-size slots
2. Free list: each free slot contains a pointer to the next free slot (intrusive linked list)
3. Thread safety: use a spinlock or atomic free-list head for lock-free allocation
4. Alignment: ensure each slot is aligned to the required boundary (typically 16 bytes for SIMD)
5. Debug features: canary values to detect buffer overflows, generation counters to detect use-after-free, tracking of allocation count for leak detection

For thread safety, I'll use an atomic compare-and-swap on the free list head. This makes both allocate and deallocate lock-free. The free list is a singly-linked list where each free block stores the pointer to the next free block.

The ABA problem applies here: a thread pops A, another thread pops A and B, pushes A back. First thread's CAS succeeds but the list has changed. I'll use a tagged pointer (counter + pointer packed in a 64-bit value) to prevent ABA.

For debug mode: write canary bytes before/after each allocation to detect overflows. Track allocated blocks in a set. On free, verify the canary and that the block was actually allocated.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <cassert>
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cstring>
#include <iostream>
#include <new>
#include <vector>
#include <thread>

// === Pool Allocator with thread-safe lock-free free list ===

class PoolAllocator {
public:
    PoolAllocator(size_t objectSize, size_t objectCount,
                  size_t alignment = alignof(std::max_align_t))
        : objectSize_(align_up(std::max(objectSize, sizeof(void*)), alignment)),
          objectCount_(objectCount),
          alignment_(alignment) {

        // Allocate aligned memory block
        size_t totalSize = objectSize_ * objectCount_;
        memory_ = static_cast<char*>(
            ::operator new(totalSize, std::align_val_t{alignment_}));

        // Initialize free list
        freeList_.store(memory_, std::memory_order_relaxed);
        for (size_t i = 0; i < objectCount_ - 1; ++i) {
            char* current = memory_ + i * objectSize_;
            char* next = memory_ + (i + 1) * objectSize_;
            *reinterpret_cast<char**>(current) = next;
        }
        // Last block points to nullptr
        *reinterpret_cast<char**>(memory_ + (objectCount_ - 1) * objectSize_) = nullptr;

        allocCount_.store(0, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

    ~PoolAllocator() {
        size_t leaks = allocCount_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        if (leaks > 0) {
            std::cerr << "[PoolAllocator] LEAK: " << leaks
                      << " objects not freed!" << std::endl;
        }
        ::operator delete(memory_, std::align_val_t{alignment_});
    }

    // Non-copyable
    PoolAllocator(const PoolAllocator&) = delete;
    PoolAllocator& operator=(const PoolAllocator&) = delete;

    void* allocate() {
        // Lock-free pop from free list
        char* head = freeList_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        while (head != nullptr) {
            char* next = *reinterpret_cast<char**>(head);
            if (freeList_.compare_exchange_weak(head, next,
                    std::memory_order_release,
                    std::memory_order_acquire)) {
                allocCount_.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);

                #ifndef NDEBUG
                // Debug: write pattern to detect use-after-free
                std::memset(head, 0xCD, objectSize_);
                #endif

                return head;
            }
        }
        return nullptr; // Pool exhausted
    }

    void deallocate(void* ptr) {
        if (!ptr) return;

        #ifndef NDEBUG
        // Debug: verify ptr is within our pool
        char* p = static_cast<char*>(ptr);
        assert(p >= memory_ && p < memory_ + objectSize_ * objectCount_
               && "Pointer not from this pool!");
        assert((p - memory_) % objectSize_ == 0
               && "Misaligned pointer!");

        // Write free pattern to detect use-after-free
        std::memset(ptr, 0xDD, objectSize_);
        #endif

        // Lock-free push to free list
        char* head = freeList_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        char* block = static_cast<char*>(ptr);
        do {
            *reinterpret_cast<char**>(block) = head;
        } while (!freeList_.compare_exchange_weak(head, block,
                    std::memory_order_release,
                    std::memory_order_relaxed));

        allocCount_.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

    size_t allocated_count() const {
        return allocCount_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

    size_t object_size() const { return objectSize_; }
    size_t capacity() const { return objectCount_; }

private:
    static size_t align_up(size_t n, size_t alignment) {
        return (n + alignment - 1) & ~(alignment - 1);
    }

    char* memory_;
    size_t objectSize_;
    size_t objectCount_;
    size_t alignment_;
    std::atomic<char*> freeList_;
    std::atomic<size_t> allocCount_;
};


// === Typed wrapper for convenience ===

template <typename T>
class TypedPool {
    PoolAllocator pool_;

public:
    explicit TypedPool(size_t count)
        : pool_(sizeof(T), count, alignof(T)) {}

    template <typename... Args>
    T* create(Args&&... args) {
        void* mem = pool_.allocate();
        if (!mem) return nullptr;
        return new (mem) T(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }

    void destroy(T* obj) {
        if (!obj) return;
        obj->~T();
        pool_.deallocate(obj);
    }

    size_t allocated() const { return pool_.allocated_count(); }
};


int main() {
    // Basic test
    TypedPool<std::pair<int, double>> pool(1000);

    auto* p1 = pool.create(42, 3.14);
    auto* p2 = pool.create(7, 2.71);
    assert(p1 && p2);
    assert(p1->first == 42 && p2->second == 2.71);
    assert(pool.allocated() == 2);

    pool.destroy(p1);
    pool.destroy(p2);
    assert(pool.allocated() == 0);

    // Multi-threaded stress test
    PoolAllocator rawPool(64, 10000, 16);
    std::atomic<int> totalAllocs{0};

    auto worker = [&]() {
        std::vector<void*> ptrs;
        for (int i = 0; i < 1000; ++i) {
            void* p = rawPool.allocate();
            if (p) ptrs.push_back(p);
        }
        totalAllocs += static_cast<int>(ptrs.size());
        for (void* p : ptrs) {
            rawPool.deallocate(p);
        }
    };

    std::vector<std::thread> threads;
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; ++i) {
        threads.emplace_back(worker);
    }
    for (auto& t : threads) t.join();

    assert(rawPool.allocated_count() == 0);
    std::cout << "Total allocations across threads: " << totalAllocs << std::endl;
    std::cout << "All pool allocator tests PASS" << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a cache-oblivious matrix transpose algorithm in C++ and compare it with the naive approach. Explain why cache-oblivious algorithms perform well without knowing the cache size.
output:
<think>
Cache-oblivious algorithms achieve good cache performance without knowing the cache size, line size, or number of cache levels. They work by recursively dividing the problem until it fits in cache, regardless of cache parameters.

Matrix transpose is a classic example. Naive transpose (nested loops) has poor locality when the matrix is large: writing to the transposed position accesses memory with stride N (cache miss per write for large N).

The cache-oblivious approach: recursively divide the matrix into quadrants. Transpose each quadrant (which eventually fits in cache), then combine. When a subproblem is small enough (fits in L1 cache), the inner loop has great locality.

Why it works: the recursive division creates a tree of subproblems. At some level, subproblems fit in L1 cache. At a higher level, they fit in L2. The algorithm adapts to ALL cache levels automatically — no tuning needed.

The recursive structure also maps well to tall-cache assumption (cache of size M holds at least M/B lines of size B, with M >= B^2). Under this assumption, the number of cache misses is O(N^2/B) for the cache-oblivious transpose, matching the optimal cache-aware version.

Comparison: naive transpose is O(N^2) cache misses (one per element in the bad dimension), cache-oblivious is O(N^2/B). For a 64-byte cache line holding 8 doubles, that's 8x fewer misses.

I'll implement both, benchmark them, and show the performance difference.
</think>

```cpp
#include <chrono>
#include <cstdlib>
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cassert>
#include <cstring>

using Matrix = std::vector<double>;

// Access element at (row, col) in a row-major NxN matrix
inline double& at(Matrix& m, int N, int row, int col) {
    return m[row * N + col];
}

inline const double& at(const Matrix& m, int N, int row, int col) {
    return m[row * N + col];
}


// === Naive transpose ===

void transpose_naive(const Matrix& src, Matrix& dst, int N) {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
            at(dst, N, j, i) = at(src, N, i, j);
        }
    }
}


// === Cache-oblivious recursive transpose ===

static constexpr int BASE_CASE = 32;  // Fits in L1 cache

void transpose_recursive(
    const Matrix& src, Matrix& dst, int N,
    int sr, int sc,   // source top-left corner
    int dr, int dc,   // dest top-left corner
    int rows, int cols // size of submatrix
) {
    if (rows <= BASE_CASE && cols <= BASE_CASE) {
        // Base case: small enough to fit in cache
        for (int i = 0; i < rows; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < cols; ++j) {
                at(dst, N, dc + j, dr + i) = at(src, N, sr + i, sc + j);
            }
        }
        return;
    }

    // Divide along the longer dimension
    if (rows >= cols) {
        int half = rows / 2;
        transpose_recursive(src, dst, N, sr, sc, dr, dc, half, cols);
        transpose_recursive(src, dst, N, sr + half, sc, dr, dc + half,
                          rows - half, cols);
    } else {
        int half = cols / 2;
        transpose_recursive(src, dst, N, sr, sc, dr, dc, rows, half);
        transpose_recursive(src, dst, N, sr, sc + half, dr + half, dc,
                          rows, cols - half);
    }
}

void transpose_cache_oblivious(const Matrix& src, Matrix& dst, int N) {
    transpose_recursive(src, dst, N, 0, 0, 0, 0, N, N);
}


// === Benchmark utility ===

template <typename Func>
double benchmark_ms(Func&& f, int iterations = 3) {
    double best = 1e18;
    for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        f();
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        double ms = std::chrono::duration<double, std::milli>(end - start).count();
        if (ms < best) best = ms;
    }
    return best;
}


bool verify_transpose(const Matrix& src, const Matrix& dst, int N) {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
            if (at(src, N, i, j) != at(dst, N, j, i)) {
                return false;
            }
        }
    }
    return true;
}


int main() {
    for (int N : {256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096}) {
        Matrix src(N * N), dst_naive(N * N), dst_co(N * N);

        // Initialize with random data
        for (int i = 0; i < N * N; ++i) {
            src[i] = static_cast<double>(rand()) / RAND_MAX;
        }

        double t_naive = benchmark_ms([&]() {
            transpose_naive(src, dst_naive, N);
        });

        double t_co = benchmark_ms([&]() {
            transpose_cache_oblivious(src, dst_co, N);
        });

        // Verify correctness
        assert(verify_transpose(src, dst_naive, N));
        assert(verify_transpose(src, dst_co, N));

        double speedup = t_naive / t_co;
        std::cout << "N=" << N
                  << "  naive: " << t_naive << "ms"
                  << "  cache-oblivious: " << t_co << "ms"
                  << "  speedup: " << speedup << "x"
                  << std::endl;
    }

    std::cout << "All correctness checks PASS" << std::endl;
    return 0;
}
```
